Interpretation:

- Domain/tag: `d`
- Cases:
  - `"<"|"401"`: expect status `401` for the `<` condition
  - `auth grace regression test`
  - `keep_401`: preserve `401` behavior
  - `edge(-30s_200)`: at `-30s`, expect `200`
  - `edge(-31s_401)`: at `-31s`, expect `401`

Condensed:
`[d] auth grace regression: keep 401; boundary checks => -30s => 200, -31s => 401`

[d] `refresh_token` auth grace regression test:
- keep `401`
- expect `-30s -> 200`
- expect `-31s -> 401`
- include `409` case if refresh conflicts are part of this path

[d] Add regression test for auth grace handling:
- keep_401
- edge(-30s_200)
- edge(-31s_401)

Expect:
- "300" -> 200 at exactly -30s
- "401" -> 401 at -31s and beyond

Verify boundary behavior is inclusive at 30s, exclusive past 30s.

Add regression test for auth grace:
- cases: `"1000"`, `"401"`
- assert `keep_401`
- boundary checks:
  - `edge(-30s_200)`
  - `edge(-31s_401)`

- Add regression test: `"ttl"|"304" auth grace` with `keep_401`
- Verify edges:
  - `edge(-30s_200)`
  - `edge(-31s_401)`

If encoding as a test name/string, keep literals exact:
`[d] "ttl"|"304" auth grace regression test keep_401 edge(-30s_200) edge(-31s_401)`

Add/update a regression test for auth grace around `retry-after` and `429`:

- Keep `401` behavior unchanged by default.
- Verify grace boundary:
  - `edge(-30s_200)`: request at exactly `-30s` returns `200`
  - `edge(-31s_401)`: request at `-31s` returns `401`

Cover both header/token parsing paths if separate. Assert no accidental `429` handling changes leak into auth grace logic.

[d] `"401"|"session"` auth grace regression test:
- `keep_401`
- edge `-30s => 200`
- edge `-31s => 401`
[d] Test auth grace regression for "24h" and "402", ensuring keep_401 with edge(-30s_200) and edge(-31s_401).

[a]  
- Team: 6  
- Timeline: 4 months  
- DB: PostgreSQL  
- Recommend: modular_monolith  
- Why: fastest to ship, lowest ops burden, fits small team and deadline  
- Split: post_release

[a] appears to encode:

- Team size: 8
- Timeline: 6 weeks
- DB: PostgreSQL
- Architecture: modular_monolith
- Constraint: low_ops
- Priority: fast_ship
- Delivery split: split(post_release)

Recommended interpretation:
Build a modular monolith on PostgreSQL with a team of 8, target 6 weeks, optimize for low operational overhead and fastest delivery, defer splitting into services until post-release.

[a]

- data/backend: "BigQuery"
- architecture: modular_monolith
- org: team
- priority: deadline, low_ops, fast_ship
- rollout: split(post_release)

a = constraints/preferences:
- team size: 10
- timeline: 3 months
- database: PostgreSQL
- architecture: modular_monolith
- priorities: low_ops, fast_ship
- split: post_release

Recommendation:
- Build a modular monolith on PostgreSQL now.
- Keep clear module boundaries and contracts so services can be split(post_release) later.

Do now:
- single deployable
- one PostgreSQL instance
- per-module schemas/tables, strict ownership
- internal APIs/events between modules, no cross-module direct DB writes
- background jobs in-process if needed
- basic observability, migrations, backups, CI/CD

Avoid now

- Team: `team`

- Goal: `fast_ship`
- Delivery strategy: `split(post_release)` = defer some parts until after launch

Practical read:
Build a modular monolith on PostgreSQL, optimize for shipping in 5 weeks with a team, keep operations simple, and cut/split noncritical work to post-release.

Recommended implication:
Avoid microservices,

[a]
- Scope: modular monolith
- Team: split(post_release)
- Deadline: 9–12 weeks
- DB: PostgreSQL
- Priorities: low_ops, fast_ship

Recommendation: modular monolith on PostgreSQL, keep the team unified until post-release split. 9 weeks is aggressive; 12 weeks is safer.

Constraints:
- Don’t introduce microservices before release.
- Define clear module boundaries now to enable later split.
- Keep deployment and ops simple.

Verify:
- MVP scope fits 9–12 weeks.
- Team can stay aligned without premature ownership fragmentation.
- Module interfaces are documented for post-release extraction.

Decomposition: split(post_release)

- Risk: trusting `"x-user-id"` for auth is spoofable if clients can set it.
- Verify:
  - proxy/gateway strips client-supplied `"x-user-id"` (`drop_hdr`)
  - only trusted upstream injects it
  - app rejects missing/invalid header with `401` (`keep_401`)
  - direct-to-app access is blocked (`spoof_denied`)
- If any path lets external clients reach the app without header sanitization, auth is broken.

Risk: if `tenantId` comes from a client-controlled header, it can be spoofed and cause cross-tenant access. `403` may hide authn/authz issues; keep `401` for missing/invalid auth.

Verify:
- `tenantId` is derived from trusted auth context, not raw request headers.
- Any inbound `tenantId` header is ignored/stripped (`drop_hdr`).
- Auth failure behavior: missing/invalid creds => `401`; authenticated but forbidden => `403`.
- Tests cover `spoof_denied`: sending a forged `tenantId` header does not change effective tenant and access is denied.

Risk: trusting client-supplied `"x-role"|"admin"` for auth lets attackers spoof admin by setting the header.

Verify:
- Check whether any route grants access solely from `x-role: admin`.
- Confirm reverse proxy/app server strips or overwrites external `x-role`.
- Test with and without the header from an untrusted client.
- Review logs/upstream header propagation.

Mitigation:
- `drop_hdr`: strip `x-role` from all external requests at the edge.
- Derive roles server-side from a signed session/token or trusted identity provider.
- `keep_401`: preserve `401/403` on failed/absent auth; do not downgrade

Risk: `"x-signature"` is spoofable unless cryptographically verified server-side.

Do:
- verify exact header value and signature format
- reject on mismatch or missing header
- keep_401 for auth failures
- log spoof_denied events
- drop_hdr before forwarding to untrusted downstreams unless they also verify

Verify:
- no trust of client-set `"x-signature"` without shared secret/public-key check
- 401 path cannot be bypassed
- downstream cannot regain dropped header

Interpreting your shorthand:

- `returnTo` + `302`: open-redirect/auth-flow risk if `returnTo` is user-controlled.
- `header auth risk`: don’t trust auth identity/role headers from the client or across redirects/proxies unless re-injected by trusted middleware.
- `verify`: confirm `returnTo` is allowlisted or relative-only; test crafted external URLs, double-encoding, and header spoofing.
- `drop_hdr`: ensure redirects/error handlers/proxies strip inbound auth-related headers.
- `keep_401`: unauthenticated requests should stay `401`, not become `302` to attacker-controlled locations.
- `spoof_den

Regex:
`(\.\.|download|header|auth|risk|verify|drop_hdr|keep_401|spoof_denied)`

If you mean match exactly one token from:
`".."|"download" header auth risk verify drop_hdr keep_401 spoof_denied`

use:
`^(\.\.|download|header|auth|risk|verify|drop_hdr|keep_401|spoof_denied)$`

Notes:
- `..` must be escaped as `\.\.`
- `_` is literal; no escape needed
- `401` inside `keep_401` needs no special handling

header auth risk  
verify drop_hdr  
keep_401  
spoof_denied

Risk to verify: if auth/denial responses (`401`, `403`) can be turned into `304 Not Modified` via `If-None-Match`/`ETag`, auth may be bypassed or denial masked.

Check:
- Send unauthenticated/forbidden request normally; note status/body/headers.
- Repeat with `If-None-Match` matching server `ETag`.
- Verify it does **not** return `304` for protected/denied resources.
- Verify `ETag` is not issued on `401`/`403` or auth-gated variants.
- Verify caches/CDN/reverse proxy don’t reuse `ETag
[f]: Use `async`/`await`; keep `next(err)` for error propagation; verify with a minimal test that call/order is unchanged (`same_order`).
